deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., T1 Turn 7 student correctly formulates cautious language; T3 Turn 8 student sharpens their rule), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the improved approach worked better. No turn explicitly asks the student to reflect on their own reasoning process after a successful correction.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor consistently applies Socratic questioning, avoids giving answers directly, scaffolds well from student responses, and maintains appropriate tone and concise communication. The only clear deduction is for 1.3.C.a: the tutor repeatedly misses opportunities to prompt explicit reflection after student improvement. No evidence of direct answer-giving, rewriting, or off-topic drift was found. The 1.3.A and 1.3.B criteria are largely met—the tutor provides process-level feedback and implicitly models reasoning rationale, though explicit meta-learning explanation of why the tutoring approach is used is minimal but present enough (e.g., T1 Turn 6: 'I won't phrase it for you—you need to do the writing').
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor excels at guided discovery across all three conversations, consistently asking diagnostic questions, building on student progress, and avoiding submission-ready output. Scaffolding is strong: the tutor builds on each student's prior responses and adjusts framing when students are confused (e.g., T2 Turn 5 refining the leading/engaged distinction). The sole deduction is under 1.3.C.a: after students successfully improve their reasoning (e.g., T1 Turn 7 cautious language, T3 Turn 8 sharpened rule), the tutor advances the conversation without pausing to ask what changed in the student's thinking or why the new approach is stronger—a missed metacognitive reinforcement opportunity present across all three transcripts. No other rubric criteria show sufficient evidence for deduction.
